title: Painting exterior timber window frames
subtitle: Prepare, prime, and paint bare or previously painted timber window frames outside
excerpt: How to repaint exterior timber window frames to protect them from rot and weather. Covers stripping back to bare wood where needed, applying the correct primer, undercoat, and finish, and the sequence for weather-resistant results.
sourceNotes: British Standard BS 6150: Code of practice for painting of buildings. English Heritage (Historic England) guidance on timber window maintenance. Collins Complete DIY Manual, Albert Jackson and David Day (1989). Crown Paints exterior preparation guidance (publicly available).

glossaryTerms.definition: A first coat applied to bare or newly exposed timber that seals the wood fibres, reduces suction, and provides a stable bonding surface for undercoat and finish. Exterior wood primer is alkyd- or acrylic-based and penetrates the wood grain. It must be applied on dry timber with no surface moisture; a wet or damp timber rejects primer.

A localised area of high resin content in softwood timber, often visible as a dark streak or a knot. If painted without pre-treatment, resin bleeds through successive coats as a brown stain and prevents the paint from adhering. Resin pockets must be sealed with an aluminium-based primer or knotting solution before priming with a standard wood primer.

content.text: Exterior timber window frames need repainting every 4 to 8 years, depending on aspect and exposure. A frame that faces south in an exposed location degrades faster than a sheltered north-facing one. The preparatory work takes far longer than the painting: stripping back to sound timber, knotting resin pockets, and applying the correct primer are the stages that determine whether the job lasts 8 years or 2. Painting over failing paint without stripping is the most common mistake.

Shave hook or paint scraper. Heat gun for thick paint removal. 80-grit, 120-grit, and 180-grit sandpaper for the sanding block. Knotting solution (for resin pockets and knots). Exterior wood primer. Exterior undercoat. Exterior gloss or satin finish. A 50 mm brush for bare sections, a 25 mm brush for glazing bars. Masking tape. A damp cloth. Wood filler or exterior flexible filler for rot-free holes and cracks.

In good weather, tap the frame with a knuckle: a solid tap means sound timber; a hollow tap or softness under finger pressure means rot. Rot must be treated (cut out soft wood and fill with a two-part wood filler, then apply a wood hardener to any remaining soft fibres) before painting begins. Use the heat gun to remove thick or flaking paint back to bare timber, working in sections. Scrape immediately after heating before the softened paint re-hardens. Use the 80-grit sanding block to remove the last traces.
Sand the stripped timber to 120-grit. Apply knotting solution to all knots and

and allow to dry. Knotting is shellac-based and dries in 10 to 15 minutes. Apply two coats to dark or resinous knots. Where paint is still sound and well bonded (passes the thumb test; no movement or flaking), it does not need stripping; light sand with 120-grit to provide a key.

to all bare timber sections with the 50 mm brush, brushing the primer well into the end grain, which is the most vulnerable point for moisture entry. Do not prime over existing paint that is well bonded; the primer is only for bare or newly exposed sections. Allow to dry fully; at least 4 hours for alkyd primer in warm conditions.
Step 4, apply undercoat and two finish coats

Apply the exterior undercoat in full to all surfaces; primed and existing painted sections alike. This unifies the surface and provides the opacity for the finish coat. Allow to dry and sand lightly with 180-grit before the finish coat. Apply the first finish coat, working quickly along the length of each section without stopping and restarting mid-run to avoid lap marks. Apply the second finish coat once the first is touch-dry (usually 4 to 6 hours for water-based products). Pay particular attention to the bottom edge of the frame and the rebate where the frame meets the glass: these are the first areas to fail if paint is thin.

On sash windows with glazing bars, paint the bars before the frame, and run the paint onto the glass by 2 mm on each side to form a seal between the glass and the putty. This seal prevents water tracking behind the putty and is not a mistake; it is the correct finish for a traditional glazed timber window.

attrs.symptom: Paint bubbling or lifting within months of application
attrs.cause: Timber was damp when painted — trapped moisture vapour forces the paint off.
attrs.fix: Paint only in dry conditions when timber has not been exposed to rain for at least 48 hours. Check with a moisture meter: timber moisture content should be below 15% before painting.

attrs.symptom: Brown resin bleed-through on new paint
attrs.cause: Knots or resin pockets were not treated with knotting solution.
attrs.fix: Allow the stained coat to cure fully, sand lightly, apply two coats of knotting solution, allow to dry, and repaint.
